metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the `--dry-run` post-install verification step with a mechanism that actually checks installed package hashes against the lock (e.g. run `pip install --require-hashes --no-deps -r <lock>` without `--dry-run` so pip enforces hashes on every package, or use `pip-audit --require-hashes -r <lock>` / a custom script that computes `sha256(wheel)` for each installed dist-info RECORD and compares against the lock).
findings.finding: The AC's post-install verification mechanism (`pip install --dry-run --require-hashes --no-deps -r <lock>`) does not actually verify that already-installed packages' wheel digests match the lock file: pip marks packages 'already satisfied' and skips digest-checking them during --dry-run, so a tampered or wrong-source pre-installed package at a matching version would silently pass.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC line 3: 'pip install --dry-run --require-hashes --no-deps -r <lock> and asserts it exits zero (every installed version satisfies the locked hashes)'
findings.suggested_fix: Remove --dry-run from the verification command: `pip install --require-hashes --no-deps -r <lock>` in an already-installed environment will either re-install and hash-verify (if hashes match, it is idempotent) or fail if a package was replaced. Alternatively, add a dedicated audit step: for each package in the lock, compute the installed wheel's hash with `pip show -f <pkg>` + sha256 of the RECORD-listed .dist-info/WHEEL file and compare against the lock's expected hash.

findings.finding: The plan asserts 'The repo already SHA-pins third-party actions in `gerrit-verify.yaml` (per its header comment)' and bases the AC lint on this being an extendable existing convention. However, `gerrit-verify.yaml` line 75 contains `actions/setup-python@v6` — a tag pin, not a SHA — directly contradicting the header comment ('All third-party actions are SHA-pinned'). The existing SHA-pin coverage is only partial (non-`actions/*` third-party actions like `lfreleng-actions/*` and `im-open/*` are SHA-pinned; `actions/setup-python@v6` is not). The AC's lint is therefore not 'extendable' from a complete existing convention — it must be newly created, and the gap in `gerrit-verify.yaml` itself is not acknowledged or scoped.
findings.location: Why section ('The repo already SHA-pins third-party actions in `gerrit-verify.yaml`') and AC line 1 ('extendable from the repo's existing SHA-pin convention in `gerrit-verify.yaml`')
findings.suggested_fix: In the AC, replace 'extendable from the repo's existing SHA-pin convention in `gerrit-verify.yaml`' with 'a new workflow-lint grep step targeting `release.yml` (note: `gerrit-verify.yaml` line 75 also has an unpinned `actions/setup-python@v6` — include or explicitly exclude that fix from this story's scope).'  In the Why section, narrow the premise to 'The repo SHA-pins some third-party actions in `gerrit-verify.yaml`' rather than asserting it is comprehensive.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the concrete assembly strategy for the multi-job release evidence artifact: either (a) have each contributing job upload a job-specific named artifact (e.g. `evidence-lint`, `evidence-build`, `evidence-mcp`) and introduce a final `release-evidence` aggregation job that downloads and concatenates them into a single artifact, or (b) use a shared workflow output/cache key with explicit ordering — and update AC 6 to name this mechanism explicitly.
findings.finding: The plan directs multiple jobs (`mcp_verify`, the build job, and the SHA-pin lint step) to each 'append' to a single shared `release-evidence.txt` artifact, but GitHub Actions artifact uploads are not atomic append operations — concurrent or sequential uploads with the same artifact name conflict or overwrite each other, leaving the assembly mechanism undefined.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC 6 (release evidence artifact); What section — 'Print and retain in release evidence'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the `python -m build` flag throughout the plan: replace `--no-build-isolation` with `--no-isolation` (the actual pypa/build CLI flag), and update the AC4 lint check string to match.
findings.finding: The plan consistently names `python -m build --no-build-isolation` (What section and AC4), but the `pypa/build` CLI flag for disabling build isolation is `--no-isolation`, not `--no-build-isolation`. `--no-build-isolation` is a `pip install` flag. Using the wrong flag will cause `python -m build` to reject the option with an unrecognised-argument error (or silently pass through in some versions, bypassing the intended enforcement entirely).
findings.location: What section ('python -m build --no-build-isolation') and AC: 'Hatchling's version is enforced end-to-end via `--no-build-isolation`'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace every occurrence of `python -m build --no-build-isolation` in the plan (What section + AC4) with `python -m build --no-isolation`. Update the AC4 lint/grep check to search for `--no-isolation` in the workflow. Note: `pip install --no-build-isolation` is the correct spelling for pip, so keep that wording only where pip is the subject.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend AC5) that exercises the positive path of the `mcp_verify → mcp_registry` two-job flow end-to-end — e.g., a dry-run release triggered in a staging/fork environment that asserts both jobs complete successfully in sequence and that `mcp_registry` consumes only the artifact uploaded by `mcp_verify`, OR explicitly defer this to a named child story with a rationale comment in the AC.
findings.finding: No acceptance criterion exercises the positive path of the new `mcp_verify → mcp_registry` two-job flow end-to-end as it will run in production (i.e., successful download + verify + upload artifact in `mcp_verify`, followed by `mcp_registry` consuming the artifact and performing login/publish). AC5 only specifies a negative/corruption test. Under E6(f), a cutover to a new code path requires at least one AC that exercises the live path end-to-end; the plan does not address this gap or explicitly defer it with rationale.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC5: 'Verified by a CI job that corrupts one byte of the pinned archive, runs the verify step, and asserts it exits non-zero.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to 'What' and 'Scope' a description of where and how the archive-corruption negative test lives (e.g., a dedicated CI workflow step, a shell script under `.github/scripts/`, triggered on PR) so the AC's 'CI job that corrupts one byte' has a concrete implementation path.
findings.finding: The AC for the MCP publisher step requires 'a CI job that corrupts one byte of the pinned archive, runs the verify step, and asserts it exits non-zero' as the verification mechanism, but this test job is never described or scoped in the 'What' or 'Scope' sections — it is an orphan verification artifact with no implementation guidance.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — MCP publisher AC ('Verified by a CI job that corrupts one byte...'); Scope section (no corresponding entry)
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'What' section under 'MCP publisher — download → verify → execute', add a fourth bullet describing the negative test: e.g., 'A CI lint/test step (e.g. `.github/scripts/test-mcp-verify.sh`) runs on every PR touching the verify logic: it downloads the archive, corrupts one byte with `dd`, and asserts `sha256sum -c` exits non-zero.' Add this script/step to the Scope file list.

findings.finding: AC7 ('`docs/releasing.md` documents the pinning policy and pin-update process') names a deliverable but provides no concrete proving command — no grep/check is specified that would produce a pass/fail signal in CI or in a review. A reviewer cannot deterministically confirm the required sections exist without an explicit check.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC7
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the workflow-lint step to run: `grep -qE '^## Pinning' docs/releasing.md || (echo 'Missing Pinning section in docs/releasing.md' && exit 1)`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the AC that the SHA-pin workflow-lint check must be written from scratch (no existing automated check exists — only a header comment convention); update the AC wording from 'extendable from' to 'implementing the same convention documented in' to set accurate expectations.
findings.finding: The AC for the SHA-pin enforcement check states it is 'extendable from the repo's existing SHA-pin convention in gerrit-verify.yaml', implying an existing automated check that can be extended. No such automated lint/grep check exists — the 'convention' is a prose header comment only (gerrit-verify.yaml:21). The plan must create this check from scratch; framing it as 'extendable' may mislead the implementor into searching for an existing script.
findings.location: AC line 1 — 'verified by a workflow-lint/grep check (extendable from the repo's existing SHA-pin convention in gerrit-verify.yaml)'
findings.suggested_fix: Change 'extendable from the repo's existing SHA-pin convention in gerrit-verify.yaml' to 'implementing the SHA-pin convention documented in gerrit-verify.yaml:21 (the convention is a comment policy, not an existing automated check — this AC creates the first automated enforcement)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the plan that `actions/upload-artifact@v7` and `actions/download-artifact@v8` must first be corrected to real published major versions (current: v4) before SHA-pinning can proceed, and add this correction to the stated scope.
findings.finding: The plan's SHA-pin task requires resolving `actions/upload-artifact@v7` (release.yml:43) and `actions/download-artifact@v8` (release.yml:60,85) to 40-char commit SHAs, but these major-version tags (`v7`, `v8`) do not correspond to any published releases of these GitHub Actions — the latest published major for both is v4. If these tags cannot be resolved to a real commit SHA, the SHA-pinning task is implementation-blocked for those steps.
findings.location: Scope section ('SHA-pin all `uses:` in release.yml'); release.yml lines 43, 60, 85
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note in the Scope section that implementation must first update `actions/upload-artifact` and `actions/download-artifact` to their current published major versions (v4 as of 2025) before resolving them to commit SHAs, since the currently referenced tags (v7, v8) do not exist in the upstream action repositories.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Remove 'wheel/sdist digests' from the 'Print and retain in release evidence' bullet in the 'What' section (or add a parenthetical matching the AC's explicit out-of-scope note) to make the two sections consistent.
findings.finding: The 'What' section lists 'wheel/sdist digests' as part of release evidence to print and retain, but the Release Evidence AC explicitly scopes these out ('Wheel/sdist SHA-256 sums are added to the same evidence artifact by Finding 3's build-once step... they are out of scope here'). This cross-section inconsistency could lead an implementer to include wheel/sdist digest steps in this story's implementation.
findings.location: 'What' section — 'Print and retain in release evidence' bullet; Acceptance Criteria — Release Evidence AC
findings.suggested_fix: Change the 'What' bullet to: 'Print and retain in release evidence: every action SHA, the Python build-tool versions, and the MCP publisher version + downloaded digest. (Wheel/sdist digests are out of scope here — owned by Finding 3.)'

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete pin-update detection mechanism (e.g., Dependabot/Renovate for the lock file; a scheduled CI job or release-note watch for the MCP publisher) and document it in the pin-update process section of `docs/releasing.md`.
findings.finding: The plan hardcodes the MCP publisher SHA-256 and the pip-tools lock file in the repo, but specifies no mechanism (Dependabot, Renovate, scheduled CI staleness check, or similar) for detecting when these pinned values need to be updated; 'Update pins through reviewed dependency-update changes' is stated as a policy without a tooling plan.
findings.location: What section — MCP publisher pin; Scope — 'A new release build lock/constraints file'; AC item 5
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `dependabot.yml` entry for `pip` covering the lock file path, and note in `docs/releasing.md` that the MCP publisher pin must be bumped manually (with a checklist step) whenever a new upstream release is verified; alternatively, add a scheduled GitHub Actions workflow that checks the upstream MCP publisher latest tag against the pinned version and opens an issue when they diverge.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare the access posture of the artifact channel in mcp_registry: either add an explicit re-digest step (echo `<pinned-sha256>  mcp-publisher` | sha256sum -c) in mcp_registry before execution, or formally declare in the plan why the GitHub Actions artifact channel is trusted without re-verification (e.g., 'internal GitHub infra; mcp_verify output SHA is pinned in the job summary; re-digest is redundant') — the current plan leaves this undeclared on the privileged side.
findings.finding: The mcp_registry job (which holds id-token: write) downloads the mcp-publisher artifact from GitHub Actions internal storage but the plan never declares whether a re-digest check is performed in that job before execution; the security contract says 'consumes only the verified artifact' without specifying how the privileged job asserts that invariant at its own boundary.
findings.location: What → Security contract — structural job split; AC: 'mcp_registry job needs: mcp_verify and consumes only the verified artifact'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a step to mcp_registry immediately after artifact download: `echo "<pinned-sha256>  mcp-publisher" | sha256sum -c --strict` (the same pinned hash used in mcp_verify). This makes the 'OIDC-privileged job never executes an unverified binary' invariant self-contained and auditable without relying on cross-job channel trust.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify AC6's 'dumps resolved action SHAs' to specify the mechanism: e.g., 'extracts the 40-char SHA values already present in the workflow YAML using grep/awk, appending them to `release-evidence.txt`' — and explicitly state whether API resolution to human-readable refs is in or out of scope.
findings.finding: AC6 states 'the SHA-pin lint dumps resolved action SHAs' into the release evidence artifact. The word 'resolved' is semantically ambiguous: it could mean (a) extracting the 40-char SHAs already present in the YAML (a grep/awk operation) or (b) resolving those SHAs to their human-readable commit metadata via the GitHub API. These are very different implementation efforts; if the executor picks (b) and the GitHub API is rate-limited or the token lacks the right scope, the lint step will fail in production.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC6: 'the SHA-pin lint dumps resolved action SHAs'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'the SHA-pin lint dumps resolved action SHAs' with 'the SHA-pin lint step extracts the 40-char SHA values from every `uses:` line in `release.yml` (via grep/awk on the YAML) and appends them to `release-evidence.txt` — no GitHub API call is required.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend the `docs/releasing.md` AC (or add an ADR/CLAUDE.md entry) to explicitly require capturing the OIDC job-split security rationale — specifically that `id-token: write` must never be co-resident with an unverified binary — as a durable, findable architectural constraint.
findings.finding: The AC for `docs/releasing.md` only requires 'pinning policy and pin-update process', leaving the security rationale for the structural `mcp_verify`/`mcp_registry` OIDC job split undocumented in any persistent artifact; the rationale currently lives only in the story's ephemeral Why/What sections.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — last AC item ('`docs/releasing.md` documents the pinning policy and pin-update process'); What section — 'Security contract — structural job split'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence to the final AC bullet: '…and documents the security rationale for the `mcp_verify`/`mcp_registry` job split (OIDC token scope isolation), so the invariant is findable by future maintainers updating the workflow.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add retry logic (e.g. `curl --retry 3 --retry-delay 5 --retry-all-errors` or a GitHub Actions `retry` wrapper step) to the MCP publisher download step in `mcp_verify`, and document the expected behavior when all retries are exhausted (fail the job with a clear error message).
findings.finding: The `curl` download of the MCP publisher archive in `mcp_verify` has no retry or backoff; a transient network failure will abort the release run with no recovery guidance.
findings.location: What section — MCP publisher download steps (curl -fsSL ... -o mcp-publisher.tar.gz)
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `curl -fsSL "<url>" -o mcp-publisher.tar.gz` with `curl -fsSL --retry 3 --retry-delay 5 --retry-all-errors "<url>" -o mcp-publisher.tar.gz` and add a note in the AC that curl's exit code must be checked (already implied by `-f`) and that exhausted retries surface a clear error message.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify gerrit-verify.yaml is actually fully SHA-pinned before relying on the 'non-release workflows already SHA-pinned' scope exclusion — gerrit-verify.yaml:75 currently uses `actions/setup-python@v6` (tag), not a SHA; either fix that gap in this story or explicitly acknowledge it as a known residual and remove the exclusion's 'already SHA-pinned' rationale.
findings.finding: The plan's scope-exclusion 'Out of scope: pinning non-release workflows already SHA-pinned' rests on the premise that gerrit-verify.yaml is uniformly SHA-pinned, but gerrit-verify.yaml line 75 uses `actions/setup-python@v6` (a tag, not a SHA) in the require-ticket job — while line 141 of the same file SHA-pins the identical action in the build-and-test job. This is a false exclusion: gerrit-verify.yaml is NOT fully SHA-pinned, so excluding it from scope on that basis silently leaves a tag-pinned action unaddressed.
findings.location: Scope section — 'Out of scope: pinning non-release workflows already SHA-pinned'; also gerrit-verify.yaml:75 vs :141
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'Out of scope: pinning non-release workflows already SHA-pinned' with 'Out of scope: pinning non-release workflows (gerrit-verify.yaml line 75 has a residual tag-pinned actions/setup-python@v6 — tracked separately)' so the exclusion is honest and the gap is surfaced rather than silently dropped.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `docs/releasing.md`'s 'Supply-chain attestations' section (line 45, which quotes `pypa/gh-action-pypi-publish@release/v1`) to the plan's edit list for that file, updating the quoted reference to reflect the new SHA pin (or noting that the human-readable tag is recorded in the trailing comment).
findings.finding: docs/releasing.md line 45 quotes the mutable `pypa/gh-action-pypi-publish@release/v1` tag verbatim in the existing 'Supply-chain attestations' section. After this story SHA-pins that action in release.yml, the quoted tag string in that docs passage becomes stale — but the plan's scope for docs/releasing.md is limited to the 'Pinning' section only, leaving this existing passage unenumerated as a consumer to update.
findings.location: Scope section, docs/releasing.md; also docs/releasing.md line 45
findings.suggested_fix: Either extend the plan's docs/releasing.md scope to include updating the inline `release/v1` quote in the 'Supply-chain attestations' section to match the new SHA-pinned reference (with human-readable tag in a comment), or add a note that the docs passage uses the human-readable form by design and will be updated to reference the trailing comment convention.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `mcp_registry` re-verifies the downloaded artifact's SHA-256 digest against the same pinned value before executing it (e.g. `echo "<pinned-sha256> mcp-publisher" | sha256sum -c --strict` immediately after downloading the artifact and before `./mcp-publisher login`), so the OIDC-privileged job never executes a binary it hasn't verified itself.
findings.finding: The plan's `mcp_verify` → `mcp_registry` artifact hand-off does not specify re-verifying the MCP publisher binary's digest inside `mcp_registry` after it downloads the artifact, leaving a gap in the chain of custody between the two jobs.
findings.location: What section — 'Security contract — structural job split'; Acceptance Criteria AC line 5
findings.suggested_fix: Add a step at the start of mcp_registry: after `actions/download-artifact`, run `echo "<pinned-sha256>  mcp-publisher" | sha256sum -c --strict` before any `./mcp-publisher` call. Store the pinned SHA-256 as a workflow-level env var or job output from mcp_verify so both jobs share a single authoritative value.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Evaluate zizmor (and/or actionlint) as the SHA-pin and persist-credentials enforcement mechanism before committing to a bespoke grep/workflow-lint step; document the decision (adopt zizmor, or justify why a custom grep is preferred over the established tool).
findings.finding: The plan specifies a bespoke 'workflow-lint/grep check' to enforce SHA-pin and persist-credentials requirements, reinventing what zizmor (a dedicated, maintained GitHub Actions security static analyzer) already provides. Zizmor detects unpinned action refs, missing persist-credentials:false, excessive permissions, and other supply-chain issues — exactly the checks this plan hand-rolls. The plan shows no awareness of this prior art.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — first two AC bullets; What section — 'Replace each action tag with a full commit SHA'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `zizmor` pre-commit hook or CI step (e.g., `uses: zizmor-community/zizmor-action@<SHA>`) configured to require SHA-pinned actions and persist-credentials:false across all workflow files. This replaces AC1/AC2's bespoke grep with a maintained, comprehensive tool and aligns with the repo's existing pre-commit pattern.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify (e.g., in a local dry-run or by citing Hatchling/PEP 517 docs) that `python -m build --no-build-isolation` correctly triggers the custom `hatch_build.py` hook and produces an equivalent wheel to the current isolated build before committing to this strategy.
findings.finding: The plan asserts that `python -m build --no-build-isolation` will correctly enforce the pre-installed Hatchling version, but this repo has a custom Hatchling build hook (`hatch_build.py`) that imports `from hatchling.builders.hooks.plugin.interface import BuildHookInterface` at build time. The plan does not verify that `--no-build-isolation` is fully compatible with Hatchling's custom hook interface — in particular, whether the custom hook's discovery and execution path behaves identically under `--no-build-isolation` vs. the default isolated build.
findings.location: What section — 'Hatchling enforcement — decision'; Acceptance Criteria — 4th bullet

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the canonical lock file path (e.g. `requirements/release-lock.txt`) explicitly in the Scope bullet rather than offering two options with 'e.g.'.
findings.finding: The lock file location is expressed as 'e.g. under `.github/` or `requirements/`' — a defaultable gap leaving the executor to make an arbitrary choice with no stated default. This can cause inconsistency with repo conventions and re-work if the author had a preference.
findings.location: Scope section, bullet: 'A new release build lock/constraints file (e.g. under `.github/` or `requirements/`)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'e.g. under `.github/` or `requirements/`' with the decided path, e.g. `requirements/release-lock.txt`, matching any existing lock-file conventions in the repo.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command for AC4's `--no-build-isolation` lint check (e.g., `grep -E '--no-build-isolation' .github/workflows/release.yml | grep -c '' | grep -qx 1`) so the verification mechanism is unambiguous.
findings.finding: AC4's proving command for the `--no-build-isolation` enforcement lint step is under-specified: the AC says 'a workflow lint/test step that fails if `--no-build-isolation` is removed' but does not state what command/script implements this check, leaving the executor to invent the mechanism.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC4: 'Hatchling's version is enforced end-to-end via `--no-build-isolation`..., with a workflow lint/test step that fails if `--no-build-isolation` is removed'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC4: 'Verified by a grep step: `grep -qE -- "--no-build-isolation" .github/workflows/release.yml || (echo "--no-build-isolation missing" && exit 1)`; this step runs in the same workflow-lint job as AC1/AC2.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out procedure to the 'What' section and `docs/releasing.md`: e.g., 'If the hash-locked env causes a release failure, the emergency path is to revert the lock file to the previous reviewed version and re-run; a forced unlock (removing --require-hashes) requires a two-reviewer approval and a follow-up re-lock within 24 hours.'
findings.finding: The plan introduces a hash-locked `--no-build-isolation` build environment as a behavioral change to the release workflow, but provides no explicit rollback or back-out procedure if the pinned build env causes a release failure (e.g., a lock file with an incompatible Hatchling version, or a toolchain regression discovered mid-release).
findings.location: 'What' section — '--no-build-isolation' decision; Scope; Acceptance Criteria

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] In the plan's 'What' or 'Scope' section, add an explicit access-class declaration for the mcp-publisher registry calls: state the OIDC namespace scope (e.g., 'authenticated via GitHub OIDC; authorizes write access to io.github.navapbc/* only; no other registry namespaces are writable') so the access posture is auditable as written.
findings.finding: The outbound mcp-publisher login/publish calls to the MCP Registry are boundary-crossing (public internet, registry server) and use OIDC authentication, but the plan never formally declares the access class of this path (e.g., 'authenticated via GitHub OIDC, scoped to io.github.navapbc/* namespace') — the access posture is described only narratively and not as an explicit contract.
findings.location: What → MCP publisher — download → verify → execute; release.yml lines 133-134 (mcp_registry job: 'Authenticate to MCP Registry via GitHub Actions OIDC')

findings.finding: AC6 (release evidence artifact) includes 'the build-once step (Finding 3) appends the wheel/sdist SHA-256 sums' — this is an undeclared cross-ticket dependency (VCDT #9). The AC can only be fully satisfied after Finding 3 lands; yet the plan declares no dependency edge, sequencing requirement, or fallback for the case where this story ships before Finding 3.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC6 ('the build-once step (Finding 3) appends the wheel/sdist SHA-256 sums')

findings.finding: The plan states 'the `id-token: write` (OIDC) scope is never co-resident with an unverified binary' and enforces this purely through step ordering (download → verify → execute within the same job). In GitHub Actions, `id-token: write` is a job-level permission: the OIDC token endpoint ($ACTIONS_ID_TOKEN_REQUEST_URL) is available to every process running in the job from its first step, regardless of step sequence. A compromised archive could call the token endpoint during the download step, before verification runs. Step ordering is a convention, not a structural enforcement boundary, so the stated security contract cannot actually be satisfied by the proposed mechanism. A job-split — download and digest-check in a job without `id-token: write`, then execute in a separate job that requires `id-token: write` — is the structurally correct mechanism to enforce the co-residency guarantee the plan names.
findings.location: What section — MCP publisher paragraph ('the `id-token: write` (OIDC) scope is never co-resident with an unverified binary'); AC5; release.yml mcp_registry job (lines 117–119, 127–134)
findings.suggested_fix: Split the `mcp_registry` job into two: `mcp_verify` (no `id-token: write`; runs curl download + sha256sum check + sets a job output `verified: true`) and `mcp_registry` (needs `mcp_verify`; has `id-token: write`; copies the verified binary via artifact or re-downloads with `--no-clobber` using the already-validated hash). Alternatively, if a full split is deemed too heavy, update the plan's language to correctly scope the guarantee as 'step-ordered defence-in-depth' rather than a structural 'never co-resident' contract — and remove the AC5 claim that this is a security isolation boundary.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
